intro: The clearest difference between Cline and Cody is lock-in — Cline has low lock-in (OSS VS Code agent, BYOK models), whereas Cody has low lock-in (Sourcegraph AI, OSS core). Beyond that, developer experience — Cline scores 4/5 against Cody's 5/5. The stated fit for both tools is similar on paper: Cline is pitched as Code Assistants / Agent Tooling tool for developers. Specializes in AI Coding Agent, while Cody is pitched as Code Assistants / Agent Tooling tool for developers. Specializes in AI Code Search. On the strengths side, Cline is cited for accelerating coding workflow, while Cody is cited for code generation and refactoring. The honest trade-off: both Cline and Cody are the wrong call for non-coding roles. Choosing between them should come down to the constraint that actually binds your workflow, not surface-level overlap. On developer experience the data shows Cline at 4/5 and Cody at 5/5, with transparency at 5/5 and 4/5 respectively.
quick_take: Cline and Cody both target Code Assistants / Agent Tooling; their data-level fit descriptions overlap, so decide based on which fit statement matches your team.
verdict_a: Choose Cline if you need a Code Assistants / Agent Tooling tool and you can work around a lower DX rating (4/5).
verdict_b: Choose Cody if you need a Code Assistants / Agent Tooling tool and developer experience matters more than Cline's trade-offs.

faq.a: Both Cline and Cody use a freemium model with a free tier, so raw pricing is close on paper. The real cost difference depends on volume, seats, and which paid features you actually need. Check current pricing on each vendor's page before deciding.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Cline to Cody?
faq.a: Migrating from Cline to Cody is feasible when their scopes overlap, but the work is in re-mapping configuration, data models, and integrations. Cline has low lock-in, so exporting state is typically straightforward. Plan for a dual-run period so you can validate parity before decommissioning.

On our developer-experience score, Cody is rated 5/5 and Cline 4/5, so Cody edges ahead. Scores like this smooth over real differences in docs, CLI feel, and SDK quality, so treat them as a starting point. Spend an afternoon in each before relying on the number.

faq.q: Is Cody a good alternative to Cline?
faq.a: Cody is a reasonable alternative to Cline when Cody fits accelerating coding workflow. If your use case matches Cline's sweet spot — accelerating coding workflow — staying with Cline is probably safer. Cline itself is not meant for non-coding roles, which is often where users start shopping for alternatives.
